World Bible · Design Direction

OTTAVIO

Italian Bakery & Delicatessen

Aesthetic direction and world‑building for a neighborhood institution in Oak Bay since 1997.

Scroll — the bread is warm...

Before we design anything...

Three generations. Torino to Oak Bay.

Monica Pozzolo's grandparents ran a pasticceria in Torino for over 40 years. Her family has operated The Italian Bakery in Victoria since 1978. In 1997, Monica and Andrew Moyer opened Ottavio in a tiny former hair salon on Oak Bay Avenue. They scraped hairspray off the light fixtures and started baking.

Today, locals line up for bread that's gone by 2pm. The staff stocks shelves from a kitchen you can see through the back. The cheese case has handwritten cards explaining every wheel. It's the kind of place people bring visitors to show them what Victoria is really like.

The website should feel like walking through that door.
The Bread Wall
Sourdough, ciabatta, olive baguette — baked before dawn
Multigrain
$7.00
Lumaca
$6.25
The Cheese Counter

Blue, aged, soft, fresh. Over 100 varieties.

Comté Marcel Petite. Tête de Moine from Jura. Murgella Burrata flown in from Puglia. Every cheese in the case has a card — where it's from, how it's made, what it tastes like. The staff will cut you a sliver to try before you buy.

The website needs this same level of detail for every product.
Handwritten tasting cards
Origin, age, flavor — right next to every wheel
Provolone, tied by hand

The shop already knows exactly what it is. The site just needs to keep up.

Every product has a story. Every staff member has a recommendation. The physical space does this naturally. The website needs to translate that — not with marketing language, but with the same specificity and care you'd get at the counter.

The Deli Case

Castellano Chorizo. Budapest Salami. Juniper Elk.

Made by Oyama. Sourced from small producers. The green labels tell you exactly what's in each one — Hungarian-style, Spanish paprika, natural smoke. No mystery meat. Every item is specific, traceable, and chosen on purpose.

E-commerce product pages should read like those green labels.
The Café

Illy coffee. Latte art in a real ceramic cup.

Not a paper cup operation. Ceramic cup, saucer, spoon. The kind of detail that tells you this place takes it seriously without having to say so. The café is where people linger — the website should have that same quality of not rushing you through.

Let people browse. Don't funnel them.
Quiche of the Day
Pizzette
Gelato & Affogato
The Pantry

25+ olive oils. Fernwood Coffee. Lavazza.

The red OLIO DI OLIVA barrel has been there for years. On top of it, Fernwood Coffee from down the road sits next to Lavazza from Torino. That's Ottavio in a single image — local and Italian, side by side, no contradiction. The curated goods section is half the reason people visit.

This is the e-commerce opportunity. Treat every product like a discovery.
The people are the brand
The website should show their faces, their hands, their pride

How Ottavio speaks

Like the person behind the counter.

Sounds like Ottavio ✓
"The Castelvetrano olives just came in. Buttery, a little sweet — try one before you buy."
Specific. Sensory. An invitation, not a pitch.
"We bake ciabatta every morning. It's usually gone by 2pm."
Just a fact. The urgency speaks for itself.
"This Barbera d'Asti goes with pretty much anything on a Tuesday night table."
Real recommendation. No tasting-note performance.
Not Ottavio ✗
"Elevate your culinary experience with our premium artisanal selection."
Says nothing. Means nothing. Pure filler.
"FRESH BREAD DAILY! 15% off your first order!!!!"
Discount language doesn't belong here.
"A cappuccino that makes you stay a little longer."
Sounds nice, says nothing. What kind? Served how? Be specific.
From the sketchbook

The visual language of Ottavio, in ink.

Oak Bay, 1997 — the world as it feels from the inside
The neighborhood, rendered in warmth
Illustrations to commission

Hand-drawn, warm, a little imperfect.

These should feel like something pinned to a cork board in an Italian kitchen. Single-weight line art in terracotta or espresso. Used for icons, section dividers, hover states, and decorative moments throughout the site.

Pane
A round sourdough loaf with score marks, seen from a slight angle. Simple line art, hand-drawn quality, single stroke weight. Warm and imperfect — not geometric.
→ Bread category icon, section divider
Formaggio
A wedge of hard cheese with a cheese knife leaning against it. Maybe a few crumbles. Hand-drawn, friendly, the kind of thing you'd doodle on a brown paper bag.
→ Cheese category icon, product page accent
Olio
An olive oil bottle with a small olive branch wrapping around it. Cork stopper, slightly tapered shape. Loose, hand-drawn — like a sketch on a recipe card.
→ Pantry/oils category, product detail accent
Caffè
A ceramic coffee cup on a saucer, seen from a slight angle. Two wisps of steam. Simple, cozy, inviting. Like a stamp on a loyalty card from a neighborhood café.
→ Café section icon, nav element
Gelato
A small bowl with two scoops of gelato, maybe a drizzle of something on top. Metal bowl, the kind they actually use at Ottavio. Loose, friendly, slightly whimsical.
→ Treats category, seasonal feature accent
La Porta
The Ottavio storefront door — wooden, with a transom window above. Wrought iron detail on the sides. The "come in" moment. Could include the stone steps.
→ Homepage hero accent, about page, 404 page
Here's what it starts to look like →

Not a store. A neighborhood you belong to.

Warmth
Every surface, color, word — warm. Never clinical, never cold.
Specificity
Real details beat marketing language every time. Say what it is.
Space
Generous whitespace. Unhurried. Let people look around.
Heritage
Three generations. Honor the history. Feel effortlessly current.
Now let's build it. — K
